coredata.dc:title: Near-infrared mediated polymer-coated carbon nanodots loaded cisplatin for targeted care management of lung cancer therapy
coredata.dc:creator: Fang, Wei
Jin, Ronghui
Mu, Wei
coredata.dc:description: The Cisplatin and its analogues are scarce for the second leading human health problem, cancer. Therefore, we have effectively engineered the PEGlyated carbon nanodots loaded with cisplatin PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV) for targeted lung cancer therapy. The PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV) confirmed by the electroscopic and spectroscopic methods. The in vitro cytotoxicity of the nanoparticles evaluated two lung cancers (A549 and HEL-299), and one non-cancerous cell line (HUVECs) were used in this study by using MTT assay. The nanoparticles are effectively killing cancer cells without affecting the nano-cancerous cells. Further, dual AO-EB fluorescent staining assay established the programmed cell death through cell morphological changes. Further, flow cytometry confirmed the induction of apoptosis by S phase arrest of the cancer cell cycle by the complexes. The results of our investigations also bear witness to CDs@Cis-Pt(IV)+NIR-NPs promising scope and potency care management for precise cancer therapy beyond platinum drugs.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vitro drug release from CDs–Pt(IV)
Cell Death by Dual AO-EB Fluorescent Staining
Induction of Apoptosis by Flow Cytometry
Cell Cycle Distribution by Flow Cytometry

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A graphical sketch of the synthesized of PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV) for lung cancer photodynamic therapy.

Characterization of CDs-NH2, CDs-Cis-Pt(IV) and PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV). A) Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV). B) Dynamic light scattering (DLS) of PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV). C) Stability studies with PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV) in water, Fetal bovine serum (FBS) and cell culture medium of RPMI-1640. D) Infrared spectra of CDs-NH2, CDs-Cis-Pt(IV) and PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV). E) UV-Vis spectra of CDs-NH2, CDs-Cis-Pt(IV) and PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV). F) Platinum release profile of CDs-Cis-Pt(IV) in PBS buffer (10 mM, pH 7.4) with or without GSH (10 mM) at 37 °C for 72 h.

MTT assay examined to evaluate the in vitro cytotoxicity of the (A) A549, (B) HEL-299 and (C) non-cancerous HUVECs cells against CDs@Cis-Pt(IV), PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV) and PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV)+NIR. In vitro cytotoxicity results of cancer cells treated with the different concentrations (50, 25, 12.5, 6.25, 3.125, and 1.56 μM) for 24 hrs dose-dependent cytotoxic activity. DMSO was used as negative (vehicle) control.

The cellular uptake was measured with fluorescence staining image using PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV) by A549 lung cells with different incubation time periods (15, 30, and 45 minutes). The Lysosome (green), nuclei (blue), PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV)with DiI (red) and merged was visualized through the confocal fluorescence microscopy. Scale bar 20 μM.

The morphological observations were examined by the dual acridine orange-ethidium bromide (AO-EB) staining methods. In A549 cells stained with AO-EB dual staining of CDs@Cis-Pt(IV), PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV) and PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV)+NIR using AO (green), and EB (red) for 24 hrs at IC50 concentration.

The apoptosis confirmed by the flowcytometry analysis with A549 cells against CDs@Cis-Pt(IV), PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV) and PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV)+NIR using Annexin-FITC staining with propidium iodide for 24 hrs at IC50 concentration. The quantification of apoptosis were examined and presented in bar diagram.

The cell cycle arrest confirmed by the flowcytometry analysis with A549 cells against CDs@Cis-Pt(IV), PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV) and PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV)+NIR using with propidium iodide for 24 hrs at IC50 concentration. The quantification of percentage of cell cycle arrest (G0-G1, S-Phase and G2/M phase) were examined and presented in bar diagram.

head.ce:title: Near-infrared mediated polymer-coated carbon nanodots loaded cisplatin for targeted care management of lung cancer therapy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Emergency, China-Japan Union Hospital of Jilin University, Changchun 130033, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: China-Japan Union Hospital of Jilin University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Emergency, China-Japan Union Hospital of Jilin University, Changchun 130033, China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Respiratory Medicine, China-Japan Union Hospital of Jilin University, Changchun 130033, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: China-Japan Union Hospital of Jilin University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Respiratory Medicine, China-Japan Union Hospital of Jilin University, Changchun 130033, China.

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: No.126, Xian Tai Road, China-Japan Union Hospital of Jilin University, Changchun 130033, Jilin Province, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: China-Japan Union Hospital of Jilin University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The Cisplatin and its analogues are scarce for the second leading human health problem, cancer. Therefore, we have effectively engineered the PEGlyated carbon nanodots loaded with cisplatin PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV) for targeted lung cancer therapy. The PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV) confirmed by the electroscopic and spectroscopic methods. The in vitro cytotoxicity of the nanoparticles evaluated two lung cancers (A549 and HEL-299), and one non-cancerous cell line (HUVECs) were used in this study by using MTT assay. The nanoparticles are effectively killing cancer cells without affecting the nano-cancerous cells. Further, dual AO-EB fluorescent staining assay established the programmed cell death through cell morphological changes. Further, flow cytometry confirmed the induction of apoptosis by S phase arrest of the cancer cell cycle by the complexes. The results of our investigations also bear witness to CDs@Cis-Pt(IV)+NIR-NPs promising scope and potency care management for precise cancer therapy beyond platinum drugs.

ce:section.ce:para: Cisplatin that contains platinum is used widely as a means of chemotherapy of cancer, but its clinical use is limited by its severe toxic effect due to indiscriminate accumulation in normal and cancerous tissues, nonspecific interactions with extra and intracellular proteins, and drug resistance [1–5]. In parallel to efforts of mitigating toxicity of cisplatin by modifying its chemical structure, an alternate approach is to use delivery vehicles that could overcome these limitations and specifically target cancerous cells. Many carrier systems such as gold, carbon, silica, and polymer have been developed, but the long-term toxicity of these carriers remains an issue [6–9]. A nature-inspired biopolymer, melanin-liked polydopamine (PDA) that has excellent biocompatibility, and free-radical-scavenging activity, has been explored as coating materials for gold nanorods, iron oxide nanoparticles, and as a substrate for the photothermal agent, but the photothermal property of PDA nanoparticles has been overlooked [10–15]. As a kind of semiconducting polymer, with a photothermal energy conversion efficiency of 40%, PDA-NPs have shown promising applications in photothermal-based cancer therapy, which is emerging as a powerful technique in cancer therapy due to localized treatment and minimal invasiveness [16–20].

Cancer is one of the deadliest human diseases with over 10 million new cases reported every year. The treatment of cancer is complex and requires targeted therapeutics along with a variety of nanoplatforms making it a thrust area of research [21–26]. Progress in biomedical fields has taken early-stage cancer diagnostics research to new dimensions with great demand for high-resolution optical imaging using organic dyes and inorganic nanoparticles (metal quantum dots) [27–31]. However, the use of various organic dyes and metal quantum dots as fluorescent probes for optical bioimaging has been hampered due to poor photostability, low water solubility, high toxicity and low biocompatibility [32–35]. Recently, fluorescent carbon nanomaterials particularly quantum/nanodots has been materialized as the most attractive probes in nanomedicine due to prolonged photostability, good water solubility, high biocompatibility and low toxicity [36–40].

Up to now, CDs-based drug nanocarriers possessing single surface property (PEGylated, negatively or positively charged) would influence their therapeutic efficiency for potential applications [15,41–43]. The PEGylated or negatively charged drug carriers can extend blood circulation time because PEGylation serves as a stealthy layer to prevent the drug carriers from rapid clearance by the immune system and negatively charged property can withstand protein adsorption originated from electrostatic repulsion [28–31]. However, PEGylation can also impede the uptake of drug carriers by cancer cells, and negatively charged drug carriers cannot easily enter into cancer cells due to electrostatic repulsion with the negatively charged cell membrane, resulting in decreased therapeutic efficiency [37–39]. On the other hand, positively charged drug carriers can facilitate the interaction with the negatively charged cell membrane to afford effective internalization by cancer cells, as well as promote the escape of drug carriers from endosome via “proton sponge” effect to avoid the drug degradation in lysosomes [44–47]. Nevertheless, positively charged drug carriers have strong nonspecific interactions with serum components so that the carriers could be phagocytized by reticular endothelial system easily, leading to rapid clearance from blood circulation [48–51]. In addition, positively charged drug carriers might also be endocytosed by normal cells through the interaction with the negatively charged cell membrane to cause undesirable side effects.

To tackle previously mentioned issues, we established the new PEGylated CDs based Cisplatin mediated nanoparticles (Fig. 1 ). The synthesized PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV) shows in vitro cytotoxicity and cellular uptake. Further, the PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV) displays the apoptotic futures against the lung cancer cells, which confirms through the AO-EB and flow cytometry analysis. Thus, this CDs-based medication nanocarrier displayed improved tumour adequacy just as low symptoms in vitro and in vivo, demonstrating noteworthy application potential in the cancer treatment.

The functionalized PEG2000 (polyethylene glycol), EDC.HCl (1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride), H2O2 (hydrogen peroxide), NHS, SA (Succinic anhydride) and Et3N (triethylamine) were attained from the TCL, China lacking refinements furthermore. Biological solvents and reagents for cell culture, RPMI 1640, DMEM and FBS were attained from cell signalling, China.

TEM image was gained at 100 kV with an FEI Technai G220 TEM (Netherlands), at 200 kV. An FT-IR spectrum was evaluated on a Spark CCD 6000 spectrometer (Thermo, Japan). UV-vis absorption spectrum was recorded with a Hitachi U-3900H spectrometer. DLS measurements were supported out by the Zetasizer Nano (Malvern, England) for analysis. Flow cytometry was conceded out by an FD DSR-II flow cytometer.

The citric acid (2.1 g) and diethylenetriamine (3.5 g) were reacted in a nitrogen atmosphere at 170 °C for 3 h. The mixture was cooled to room temperature before adding a certain amount of water. The solution was dialyzed against water for 48 h by using a dialysis membrane with MWCO of 3.5 kDa followed by frozen drying.

Cis-[PtCl2(NH3)2] (300.0 mg) was suspended in water (7.5 mL), and then hydrogen peroxide (10.5 mL, 30 wt %) was added dropwise at 50 °C. After a stirring period of 2 h, the mixture was cooled to room temperature. The solvent was reduced to about 2 mL by rotary evaporation, and the resulting solution was kept at 0 °C overnight. The pale yellow crystals were collected by filtration and washed with cold water and cold ether, and then dried in vacuo. Yield: 53%.

C,t,c-[PtCl2(OH)2(NH3)2] (120.0 mg) and succinic anhydride (36.0 mg) were dissolved in DMSO (2.0 mL), and the solution was stirred at room temperature for 24 h. The reaction product was frozen dried, and the attained solid was washed by cold ethanol and diethyl ether to give (SA-PtCl2(OH)(NH3)2).

The cisplatin prodrug 100 mg of SA-PtCl2(OH)(NH3)2 and PEG2000 were dissolved with CHCl3 and the solution was stirred at RT for 12 hrs. The mixture was cooled and then dried with per ether and CHCl3 to afford PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV)-NPs. The final product was kept into the overnight at 0 °C. Further, the final product was confirmed by TEM, IR, and UV-Vis spectroscopy methods and the size of PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV)-NPs confirmed by DLS analysis.

The quantum yields of CDs and CDs–Pt(IV) were determined by using quinine sulfate as a reference (54%, 0.1 M H2SO4 as solvent). The formula for calculating quantum yield (QY) is QYx=QYref(Ix/Iref)(ηx 2/ηref 2)(Aref/Ax), where x represents the sample, ref represents the standard compound quinine sulfate, I mean integrated fluorescence intensity, η means refractive index for the solvent, and A indicates the absorbance at the excitation wavelength (less than 0.1 to decrease reabsorption effects).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro drug release from CDs–Pt(IV)
ce:section.ce:para: CDs–Cis-Pt(IV) (8.0 mg) was dissolved in PBS buffer (1.0 mL, 10 mM, pH 7.4) containing 0 mM or 10 mM GSH, and transferred into a dialysis tube (MWCO of 1 kDa), followed by dialysis against PBS buffer (69 mL, containing 0 mM or 10 mM GSH) at 37 °C in the dark. At each designated time point, a certain volume of the release medium was withdrawn and the same amount of new buffer solution was added to the original release medium.

The MTT assays were examined previously reported procedures [52–54]. The lung cancer cells A549 and HEL-299 were cultured in 96 well plates. Each cell density 1 × 103 each well, in 100 μL of RPMI 1640 medium and DMEM for HUVEC cells per well for 24 hours. The next day, the medium is replaced by fresh medium and CDs@Cis-Pt(IV), PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV)-NPs and PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV)-NPs + NIR with different concentrations and incubated with 24 hours. After 24 hours the OD value calculated through the spectromax microplater at λ = 540 nm.

A549 cells obtained from the American Tissue Culture Collection (ATCC) were developed with RPMI 1640 supplemented by 10 % FBS and 1 % penicillin and streptomycin in 5 % CO2 at 36 °C. A549 cells are seeded in a 48-well plate at a thickness of 103 cells for every well and refined for 24 hours. The green-discharging fluorescent colour, Lysosome was appended to the PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV)-NPs for estimation earlier the evaluation of cell uptake efficiency. A549 cells were seeded in a 24-well plate at a thickness of 103 cells for each well and brooded with 50 μg mL−1 of PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV)-NPs for 15, 30, and 45 min. The cells were cleaned with PBS recoloured with FITC, Lysosome and DAPI. A confocal laser checking magnifying instrument (CLSM, Zeiss LSM 780, Japan) was utilized for watching and imaging the dissemination of PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV)-NPs.

4 × 103 of A549 cells were seeded in 24-well plates and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. CDs@Cis-Pt(IV), PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV)-NPs and PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV)-NPs + NIR (IC50 concentrations) were incubated with A549 cells. After incubation of 24 h, AO (100 μg/mL) and EB (100 μg/mL) were added to each well (500 μL). After 5 min, the cells were visualized via a fluorescence microscope (Olympus, BX-60, Japan), and the cell death was measured three random fields of the microscope [55–57].

The flow cytometry analysis with the fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) Annexin V Apoptosis Detection Kit (Multi Sciences, China) used to determine the A549 cells apoptotic ratio. The cells were composed by trypsinization, and washed with twice and resuspended in 500 μL 1 × binding buffer with 5 μL of FITC Annexin V and 10 μL of PI. After 15 min, the samples were subjected to analysis by flow cytometry. The outcomes were analysed with the BD FACS Calibur™ system [58–60].

A549 cells were seeded into 6 well plates incubated and allowed to attach for 24 h. Then, fresh media containing (IC50 concentrations) of CDs@Cis-Pt(IV), PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV)-NPs and PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV)-NPs + NIR were added and further incubated for another 24 h. The untreated cells were included as the control. After drug treatment, the cells were centrifuged at 1000 RPM for 5 min and washed with cold PBS. The cells were fixed with 75% ethanol at 4 °C overnight. The cells were then collected and washed twice with PBS. Thereafter, the cells were stained with a solution containing PI (50 μg/mL) and incubated 30 min in the dark condition. Cell cycle distribution was then analysed with a BD FACS CantoTM II flow cytometer [58,61,62].

The data are expressed as means ± standard deviation from several separate experiments. Statistical analyses were performed using Student’s T-test and a value of P < 0.05 was considered statistically significant.

After synthesized PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV) nanoparticles, we were examined IR, UV-Vis and TEM analysis for the confirmation of PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV) nanoparticles. The results undoubtedly display the formation of PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV). The TEM analysis image illustrated in Fig. 2
A. This analysis reveals that the formation of PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV). The clear morphology and well-organized morphology of the PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV) confirms the formation of the nanoparticle with a size of ∼135 ± 3.2 nm. Further, we confirmed the size through the DLS hydrodynamic parameters, which also confirms that the formation nanoparticles and the size of PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV) is 143.1 ± 1.2 nm with the significant PDI value and are 0.133 (Fig. 2B). In addition to that, we have examined the stability of the PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV) nanoparticles formation by the DLS analysis using water, FBS and REMI 1640 medium (Fig. 2C). The studies established a significant variation in the size between 7 days of examinations. The IR spectroscopy results revealed the coordination mode of the CDs-NH2, CDs@Cis-Pt(IV) and PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV) (Fig. 2D). The disappearance of the CDs-NH2 formation, we can conclude the formation of CDs@Cis-Pt(IV). Further, the disappearance of the carboxylic moiety assumed that the establishment of PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV). The succinic anhydride carboxylic acid reacts with PEG2000, and we attained from the PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV). Finally, UV-Vis spectroscopy analyzed to confirm the structural absorbance for CDs-NH2, CDs@Cis-Pt(IV), and PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV), which indicated the new band formation at the 460 nm. Also, the 320 nm and 410 nm reveal the platinum MLCT transition (Fig. 2E). The fluorescence quantum yield of CDs was calculated to be 25.5% under an excitation wavelength of 360 nm by using quinine sulfate (0.1 M H2SO4 as a solvent, quantum yield 54%) as the reference. The fluorescence quantum yield of CDs–Pt(IV) was calculated to be 17.9%, revealing its feasibility for bioimaging application. To investigate the cisplatin release behaviour from CDs–Pt(IV), CDs–Pt(IV) was dialyzed without or with glutathione (GSH, 10 mM, a similar concentration inside cancer cells) at pH 7.4 for 72 h. As shown in Fig. 2F, only a slight amount of cisplatin(IV) prodrug was released from CDs–Pt(IV) without GSH, which is presumably due to the hydrolysis of the linkage between cisplatin(IV) prodrug and CDs. Upon the incubation with 10 mM GSH, more than 86% of cisplatin was released after 72 h, confirming the controlled release of cisplatin from CDs–Pt(IV) under reductive condition.

After successful combinations of CDs@Cis-Pt(IV), PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV) and PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV)+NIR, we performed the MTT assay examinations to evaluate the cytotoxicity of the CDs@Cis-Pt(IV), PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV) and PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV)+NIR to two lung cancer cell lines (A549 and HEL-299). Next, the treatment with the medications for 24 h, the viability of the cells was monitored, and the half inhibitory concentration (IC50) was obtained from the dose-dependent curve (Fig. 3 ). Remarkably, A549 and HEL-299 display substantial improvement cytotoxicity of the cancer cells. For intense, in A549 cell lines, the IC50 of 27.03 ± 2.30, 12.87 ± 1.56 and 6.27 ± 1.25 for CDs@Cis-Pt(IV), PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV) and PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV)+NIR respectively. In HEL-299 cell lines, the IC50 of 24.5 ± 3.12, 16.45 ± 0.97 and 6.15 ± 2.87 for CDs@Cis-Pt(IV), PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV) and PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV)+NIR respectively. The enhanced cytotoxicity of the polymeric conjugation into carbon dots entire into the tumour cells. The hydrophilic molecules dispense the aqueous layer via lipid bilayer of the cell membrane penetrations. Additionally, Fig. 3 shows the A549 and HEL-299 cells viability with IC50 concentration of CDs@Cis-Pt(IV), PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV) and PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV)+NIR. Further, the selectivity of the CDs@Cis-Pt(IV), PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV) and PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV)+NIR was examined by the MTT assay using HUVEC cells. It displayed that the nanoparticles are without affecting normal cancer cells, it kills only cancer cells.

A confocal laser scanning microscopy was used to investigate the cell uptake of lysosome loaded PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV) wit DiI. Fig. 4 shows the growing number of nanoparticles adopted in A549 lung cancer cells by (15, 30, and 45 minutes) incubation period (Fig. 4). Undoubtedly, the efficiency of cellular uptake for PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV) was much higher than 45 minutes incubations, which suggests that platinum(IV) to (II) can improve the efficacy of cellular uptake of PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV) via cell membrane penetrations through the lysosome. The cellular uptake studies Fig. 4 displayed that the endocytic pathway.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell Death by Dual AO-EB Fluorescent Staining
ce:section.ce:para: After having identified the cancer cell growth inhibition potency of the nanoparticles by MTT, we were interested to investigate the potency of the nanoparticles in promoting cancer cell death in a programmed manner, since the anticancer drugs are ranked as potent when it detects cancer cells and bring out their death by apoptosis. Dual AO-EB staining technique is a qualitative method to comprehend apoptosis and categorize live, early apoptotic, late apoptotic and necrotic cells by accounting cell nuclear morphological changes by fluorescent images. AO can permeate the intact membrane of normal and early apoptotic cells and bind DNA then fluorescing green, which is uniform in case of former and as patches in latter due to chromatin condensation. In contrast, EB is permeable only to the injure membranes of late apoptotic and dead cells and fluoresce as bright orange patches by binding with DNA fragments of apoptotic moieties and as uniform orange in necrotic cells which have nuclear morphologies as viable cells. The cells were treated with the CDs@Cis-Pt(IV), PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV), and PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV)+NIR for 24 h at IC50 concentrations. Since the test compounds induced apoptotic cell death by cell membrane blebbing / shrinkage, EB entered into the cell and produced greenish orange stain. As shown in Fig. 5 , the appearance reddish-orange ﬂuorescence with fragmented chromatin after treatment of A549 and HEL-299 cells treated with CDs@Cis-Pt(IV), PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV), and PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV)+NIR suggesting that the PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV)+NIR mostly induced apoptosis in A549 lung cancer cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Induction of Apoptosis by Flow Cytometry
ce:section.ce:para: Apoptosis can be reckoned as an important impediment for a cell to become a developing cancer cell. Qualitative substantiation of apoptosis induction property of the nanoparticles by AO-EB dual staining approach prompted us to clarify and quantify the apoptotic cancer cells by flow cytometry using Alexa Fluor 488 Annexin V combined with propidium iodide (PI) staining assay in A549 cells. Annexin V is a cell membrane-impermeable protein having a very high affinity towards phosphatidylserine (PS). PS is a cell cycle signalling phospholipid located inner side of the membrane of a healthy cell but is reverted to the outer membrane at the time of apoptosis. So early and late apoptotic cells were identified as green images in the four quadrants diagram of flow cytometry by binding of fluorescent conjugated Annexin V with exposed PS. PI is a DNA intercalating fluorescent dye also impermeable to the intact cell membrane but can penetrate through the damaged membrane of late apoptotic and early necrotic cells and binds to DNA fluorescing red. The cells at different stages i.e live cells, early apoptotic cells and late apoptotic cells were quantified in Annexin V-/PI-, Annexin V+/PI- and Annexin V+/PI + quadrants respectively using fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS) methodology. As projected in Fig. 6 , the incubation of CDs@Cis-Pt(IV), PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV) and CDs@Cis-Pt(IV)+NIR with A549 cells conspicuously induced apoptosis (∼30 %). It is worth to note that the titled fabricated induce apoptosis even at deficient concentrations which is less than their IC50. In comparison with control, the cell population was higher (∼30 %) in annexin V(+)/PI(-) (upper left) quadrant indicating the induction of early apoptosis. This effect was ascertained to be high for CDs@Cis-Pt(IV)+NIR than the rest of the CDs@Cis-Pt(IV) and PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV) analogous with the results of MTT, AO-EB staining and nuclear staining assays (Fig. 6).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell Cycle Distribution by Flow Cytometry
ce:section.ce:para: Cell cycle proceeds through different phases called as G0-G1, S, and G2-Mitosis. The efficacy of the anticancer agent is based on its capability to disrupt cancer cell cycle in the aforementioned phases. The complexes were examined by flow cytometry to validate its impact on disrupting the A549 cell cycle. In the absence of test complexes, 72.8 % of cells were at G0-G1 phase and 18.6 % cells were found at S phase. In the presence of test complexes, the distributions of cells in G0-G1 phase were decreased to 61.4 % to 31.6 %, and 37.5 % for CDs@Cis-Pt(IV), PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV), PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV)+NIR respectively and increased to 22.7 % of (CDs@Cis-Pt(IV)), 23.3 % (PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV)), and 34.4 % (PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV)+NIR) in S phase (Fig. 7 ). Accumulation of cells in S phase evidenced that the complexes arrest the cell cycle progression in S phase. The results were suggested that PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV)+NIR shows higher cellular phase accumulation in A549 lung cancer cells.

In conclusion, we have effectively synthesized PEG@CDs-Pt(IV)-NPs, and various analytical and spectroscopic methods (IR, UV-Vis, TEM, and DLS) have confirmed the above. The DLS measurements verified the stability of the PEG@CDs-Pt(IV)-NPs using different day intervals. Next, we established the in vitro cytotoxicity of the CDs@Cis-Pt(IV), PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV), and PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV)+NIR using A549 and HEL-299 by MTT assay. The MTT assay results revealed that the compounds are effectively killing the cancer cells without killing healthy cancer cells (HUVECs). Further, we examined the cellular uptake of the CDs@Cis-Pt(IV), PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV), and PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV)+NIR nanoparticles shows after 30 minutes duration it release the drugs into the cell membrane forming the endocytic pathway. Also, establish the morphological changes using AO-EB dual staining methods. The compounds effectively induce apoptosis. Additionally the apoptosis the CDs@Cis-Pt(IV), PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV) and PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV)+NIR nanoparticles confirmed through the flow cytometry, after 24 hrs with using IC50 concentration, the PEG-CDs@Cis-Pt(IV)+NIR efficiently induce apoptosis in A549 cells. These observations are undoubtedly displayed using this type of nanoparticles for future anticancer developments.
Wei Fang: Methodology, Resources, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Ronghui Jin: Methodology, Resources, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Wei Mu: Supervision.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Association of cisplatin nephrotoxicity with patient characteristics and cisplatin administration methods
Cancer Chemotherapy and Pharmacology

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D.J. Stewart, C.S. Dulberg, N.Z. Mikhael, M.D. Redmond, V.A.J. Montpetit, R. Goel, Association of cisplatin nephrotoxicity with patient characteristics and cisplatin administration methods, Cancer Chemotherapy and Pharmacology. 40 (1997) 293–308. doi:10.1007/s002800050661.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preclincial Evaluation of Intravesical Cisplatin Nanoparticles for Non-Muscle-Invasive Bladder Cancer
Clinical Cancer Research

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Kates, A. Date, T. Yoshida, U. Afzal, P. Kanvinde, T. Babu, N.A. Sopko, H. Matsui, N.M. Hahn, D.J. McConkey, A.S. Baras, J. Hanes, L. Ensign, T.J. Bivalacqua, Preclincial Evaluation of Intravesical Cisplatin Nanoparticles for Non-Muscle-Invasive Bladder Cancer, Clinical Cancer Research. (2017) clincanres.1082.2017. doi:10.1158/1078-0432.CCR-17-1082.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Lipid-Coated Cisplatin Nanoparticles Induce Neighboring Effect and Exhibit Enhanced Anticancer Efficacy
ACS Nano

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Guo, Y. Wang, L. Miao, Z. Xu, C.M. Lin, Y. Zhang, L. Huang, Lipid-Coated Cisplatin Nanoparticles Induce Neighboring Effect and Exhibit Enhanced Anticancer Efficacy, ACS Nano. 7 (2013) 9896–9904. doi:10.1021/nn403606m.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Lipid-coated nanoscale coordination polymers for targeted cisplatin delivery
RSC Advances.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R.C. Huxford-Phillips, S.R. Russell, D. Liu, W. Lin, Lipid-coated nanoscale coordination polymers for targeted cisplatin delivery, RSC Advances. 3 (2013) 14438–14443. doi:10.1039/c3ra42033g.
